stem: According to ONI art. 142, which ordinance do the definitions of "risk analysis", "safety report" and "duty of diligence" refer to?
choices: To the ordinance of 14 March 1994 on the construction of vessels (OCEB). | To the Inland Navigation Ordinance (ONI) itself. | To the Navigation Regulations for Lake Geneva (RNL).
explanation: ONI art. 142 refers, for these concepts, to the articles of the OCEB (ordinance of 14 March 1994 on the construction of vessels).

stem: Among these statements about the definitions in ONI art. 142, which one is FALSE?
choices: The "duty of diligence" is defined in ONI art. 142 without reference to another ordinance. | The "safety report" is a report within the meaning of art. 2, let. e, and art. 17, para. 3, OCEB. | The "expert examination report" is a report within the meaning of art. 2, let. f, OCEB.
explanation: According to ONI art. 142, let. e, the "duty of diligence" is defined by reference to art. 5 OCEB, and not without reference.

stem: According to ONI art. 142, how are the "experts" defined within the meaning of the special provisions of this section?
choices: As the persons referred to in art. 5a, para. 1, let. a, OCEB. | As the officers of the competent authority of the place of mooring. | As the members of a recognized classification society.
explanation: According to ONI art. 142, let. c, the "experts" are the persons referred to in art. 5a, para. 1, let. a, OCEB.

stem: What is a “pushed convoy” according to the ONI?
choices: An assembly of non-propelled vessels joined together into a rigid unit, pushed by at least one motorised vessel | An assembly of non-propelled vessels towed by at least one motorised vessel | Any group of pleasure craft navigating together
explanation: ONI art. 2: the pushed convoy is a rigid unit that is pushed; if towed = towed convoy.

stem: Within the meaning of the ONI, what does the term "motorized vessel" designate?
choices: A vessel with mechanical propulsion | A non-propelled vessel towed by another vessel | A floating device such as a dredger or a crane
explanation: ONI art. 2: a "motorized vessel" is a vessel with mechanical propulsion.

stem: According to RNL art. 71, does navigation in restricted visibility also concern situations that are not explicitly listed?
choices: Yes, it also covers any other conditions limiting visibility in the same manner. | No, only the conditions specifically cited (mist, fog, etc.) are covered. | Yes, but only conditions caused by smoke from a fire.
explanation: RNL art. 71 extends the concept to any other conditions limiting visibility in the same manner.

stem: According to RNL art. 71, what is considered navigation in restricted visibility?
choices: Any navigation, by day as by night, in mist, fog, drizzle, snow or heavy squalls with rain. | Only navigation at night in thick fog. | Only navigation by day when the rain is accompanied by strong wind.
explanation: RNL art. 71 defines restricted visibility as any navigation, by day as by night, in mist, fog, drizzle, snow or heavy squalls with rain.

stem: Among these statements about restricted visibility within the meaning of RNL art. 71, which one is FALSE?
choices: Navigation in restricted visibility applies only to navigation at night. | Navigation in snowy weather is considered navigation in restricted visibility. | Navigation in drizzly weather is considered navigation in restricted visibility.
explanation: RNL art. 71 states that restricted visibility applies both by day and by night, and not only at night.

stem: According to RNL art. 16, which statement is correct concerning events on the water?
choices: When authorising an event, the authority may permit derogations from certain provisions of the Regulation provided that the safety of navigation is not affected | Nautical events never require prior authorisation | In the case of a binational event, a single authority decides without informing the other
explanation: RNL art. 16, para. 3, allows derogations from certain provisions provided that the safety of navigation is not affected.

stem: Under which condition in particular is authorisation for a nautical event granted according to RNL art. 16?
choices: If the prescribed civil liability insurance has been taken out | If the event takes place exclusively in Swiss waters | If all participants hold a category A permit
explanation: RNL art. 16, para. 2, let. b, requires that the prescribed civil liability insurance has been taken out.

stem: When two vessels follow directly opposite courses with a risk of collision, how must they manoeuvre?
choices: Each turns to starboard so as to pass port to port | Each turns to port so as to pass starboard to starboard | Both maintain their course and speed without any manoeuvre
explanation: RNL art. 62, let. c, provides that each vessel turns to starboard so as to pass port to port.

stem: When two vessels follow crossing courses with a possible danger of collision, which one must give way?
choices: The vessel that sees the other on its starboard side | The vessel that sees the other on its port side | The faster of the two vessels
explanation: According to RNL art. 62, let. b, the vessel that sees the other on its starboard side must give way.

stem: According to RNL art. 64, which statement is FALSE concerning sailboards and kitesurfs?
choices: Other vessels must give way to sailboards and kitesurfs | Sailboards and kitesurfs give way to all other vessels | Every vessel must give way to a vessel unable to manoeuvre that signals its presence
explanation: RNL art. 64, para. 2, let. f, states on the contrary that sailboards and kitesurfs give way to all other vessels.

stem: Faced with a vessel unable to manoeuvre that signals its presence, what must every other vessel do?
choices: Keep clear of that vessel | Maintain its course and speed | Overtake it on the starboard side
explanation: RNL art. 64, para. 1: every vessel must keep clear of a vessel unable to manoeuvre that signals its presence.

stem: According to the right-of-way rules, which vessel(s) must a sailing vessel give way to?
choices: Passenger vessels having right of way and towed convoys | Cargo vessels | Rowing boats
explanation: According to RNL art. 64, para. 2, the sailing vessel gives way in particular to passenger vessels having right of way, towed convoys and cargo vessels, but not to rowing boats.

stem: When meeting, which vessels must every vessel — apart from priority passenger vessels and towed convoys — keep clear of?
choices: Cargo vessels | Pleasure craft | Sailing sport boats
explanation: RNL art. 64, para. 2 let. b: it keeps clear of cargo vessels.

stem: According to RNL art. 84, under what conditions are the boarding and disembarking of passengers authorised?
choices: Only at facilities permitted by the competent authority and after the operator has ensured that the vessel is properly moored | At any point on the shore as soon as the vessel slows down | Without mooring if the safety of the passengers appears to be guaranteed
explanation: RNL art. 84, para. 1, authorises boarding and disembarking only at permitted facilities and after verification of the mooring and of safety.

stem: Concerning the order of use of landing stages, which statement is FALSE according to RNL art. 84?
choices: Boarding passengers may access the landing stage at the same time as passengers are disembarking | A passenger may not board or disembark without the formal authorisation of the operator or their agent | The waiting rule does not apply to landing stages equipped with independent entrances and exits
explanation: RNL art. 84, para. 4, requires on the contrary that boarding passengers access the landing stage only after the disembarking passengers have left it, except where there are independent entrances/exits.

stem: Which radio channel is designated as the alert channel (Lake Distress) in the equipment of passenger vessels?
choices: Channel 16 | Channel 12 | Channel K
explanation: RNL art. 86a, para. 1, let. c, designates channel 16 as the alert channel (Lake Distress), channel 12 being operational and channel K being the command coordination channel.

stem: Among the communication and information equipment, which items apply only to passenger vessels making an international trip?
choices: The radar device | The rate-of-turn indicator | The radio installation capable of transmitting on alert channel 16
explanation: According to RNL art. 86a, para. 2, only the requirements of para. 1, let. a (radar) and b (rate-of-turn indicator) apply solely to international trips.

stem: What minimum buoyancy in fresh water must the collective lifesaving devices of a passenger vessel provide per person?
choices: At least 750 N per person | At least 500 N per person | At least 100 N per person
explanation: According to RNL art. 86f, para. 2, let. b, the buoyancy in fresh water must be at least 750 N per person.

stem: Concerning the inflatable collective lifesaving devices on board passenger vessels, which statements are correct?
choices: They must consist of at least two separate air compartments | They must maintain a stable trim even if only half of the air compartments are inflated | They must inflate only by manual command, never automatically
explanation: RNL art. 86f, para. 3, requires at least two separate compartments and a stable trim even with half of the compartments inflated, the inflation being able to be automatic or manual.

stem: What minimum buoyancy must individual lifesaving devices have according to ONI art. 134 (excluding the exception of art. 134a)?
choices: At least 75 N. | At least 100 N. | At least 50 N.
explanation: ONI art. 134, al. 2 sets the buoyancy of individual devices at at least 75 N.

stem: According to ONI art. 134, above which thresholds must a pleasure or sport boat be provided with an additional lifesaving device that can be thrown into the water?
choices: A power output greater than 30 kW, or a sail area exceeding 15 m2 for sailing boats. | A power output greater than 15 kW, or a sail area exceeding 30 m2. | A power output greater than 50 kW, or a sail area exceeding 10 m2.
explanation: ONI art. 134, al. 5 applies to boats of more than 30 kW and to sailing boats whose sail area exceeds 15 m2.

stem: Which of these devices are INDIVIDUAL lifesaving appliances within the meaning of the ONI?
choices: Lifejackets with collars | Lifebuoys | Boarding life rafts
explanation: ONI art. 134: lifejackets with collars and lifebuoys = individual; boarding life rafts are collective.

stem: What minimum buoyancy must an individual lifesaving device have (general case)?
choices: At least 75 N | At least 750 N | At least 50 N
explanation: ONI art. 134, al. 2: at least 75 N (except for vessels referred to in art. 134a).

stem: For a loaded cargo boat ready to depart to be considered as having sufficient stability, what conditions does the text require?
choices: The heeling angle does not exceed 5 degrees and the side of the deck, at its lowest point, does not dip into the water. | The heeling angle does not exceed 15 degrees and the deck may dip up to 5 cm. | The heeling angle does not exceed 10 degrees, regardless of the position of the deck.
explanation: According to ONI art. 143a, al. 2, the heeling angle must not exceed 5 degrees and the lowest side of the deck must not dip into the water.

stem: What does ONI art. 143a provide regarding the metacentric height of the loaded boat ready to depart?
choices: It must not be less than 1 m. | It must not be less than 0.5 m. | It must not exceed 1 m.
explanation: ONI art. 143a, al. 2 specifies that the metacentric height of the loaded boat ready to depart must not be less than 1 m.

stem: According to ONI art. 144, what is the prescribed freeboard for floating equipment?
choices: 90 cm in zone 2 and 45 cm in zone 3. | 30 cm in zone 2 and 15 cm in zone 3. | 100 cm in zone 2 and 50 cm in zone 3.
explanation: ONI art. 144, al. 5 sets the freeboard of floating equipment at 90 cm in zone 2 and 45 cm in zone 3.

stem: According to ONI art. 144, what is the freeboard of an undecked cargo boat in zone 2?
explanation: ONI art. 144, al. 2, let. b sets the freeboard of undecked boats at 100 cm in zone 2.

stem: Which of these statements about the collision bulkhead is FALSE according to ONI art. 146?
choices: It may include doors and hatches if they are watertight. | It must be watertight and built from one side of the hull to the other. | It must be built from the bottom of the hold up to the deck.
explanation: ONI art. 146, al. 5 prohibits any door, hatch or opening in the collision bulkhead.

stem: According to ONI art. 146, with how many bulkheads must a cargo boat at least be fitted?
choices: At least one collision bulkhead and two bulkheads for the engine room. | At least three collision bulkheads at the bow. | At least one bulkhead for the engine room only.
explanation: ONI art. 146, al. 2 requires at least one collision bulkhead and two bulkheads for the engine room.

stem: Regarding the «CE» marking on sport boats placed on the market, what does ONI art. 148g say?
choices: The obligation to affix the «CE» marking is not applicable, but if it has already been affixed in accordance with the EU provisions, it may remain affixed. | The «CE» marking is mandatory for all sport boats placed on the market. | The «CE» marking must be removed if it was affixed in accordance with the EU provisions.
explanation: ONI art. 148g, al. 2 provides that the «CE» marking is not mandatory but may remain affixed if it has already been affixed in accordance with the EU provisions.

stem: In which language must the documentation relating to a sport boat be presented to the competent authority according to ONI art. 148g?
choices: In an official language of Switzerland or in English. | Exclusively in German. | Only in the manufacturer's language.
explanation: ONI art. 148g, al. 6 requires that the documentation be presented in an official language of Switzerland or in English.

stem: ONI art. 150 allows the crew to be reduced by one deckhand, on boats of less than 350 tonnes and in good visibility. Which of these conditions is required?
choices: The travel time between the points of departure and arrival does not exceed 45 minutes. | The travel time between the points of departure and arrival does not exceed 90 minutes. | The boat must transport exclusively dangerous goods.
explanation: ONI art. 150, al. 4, let. b sets a maximum of 45 minutes of travel time allowing this reduction.

stem: According to ONI art. 150, what is as a general rule the crew complement of a motorized cargo boat with a loading capacity greater than 1000 t?
choices: 2 deckhands. | 1 deckhand. | 3 deckhands.
explanation: ONI art. 150, al. 2, let. a provides for 2 deckhands for motorized boats of more than 1000 t loading capacity.

stem: According to ONI art. 163, in which case is a construction number not necessary?
choices: For boats that are not built by professionals. | For all pleasure boats, without exception. | For priority passenger boats.
explanation: ONI art. 163, al. 1, let. g provides that a construction number is not necessary for boats not built by professionals.

stem: According to ONI art. 163, the granting of other derogations (not listed) requires in principle:
choices: The consent of the OFT. | The agreement of the Federal Council. | No additional authorization.
explanation: ONI art. 163, al. 3 provides that other derogations may only be granted with the consent of the OFT.

stem: According to ONI art. 96a, in which case may a collective navigation permit be used?
choices: For breakdown-assistance and towing trips. | For regular commercial passenger transport. | For any private leisure trip of the owner.
explanation: ONI art. 96a, al. 3, let. a authorizes the use of the collective permit for breakdown-assistance and towing trips.

stem: What minimum third-party liability insurance coverage is required for the issuance of a collective navigation permit according to ONI art. 96a?
choices: Two million francs per accident. | One million francs per accident. | Five million francs per accident.
explanation: ONI art. 96a, al. 1, let. c requires a minimum coverage of two million francs per accident.

stem: What does the text confirm about the bowline when it is not under tension?
choices: It is easy to undo, even when wet and after having been subjected to a very strong pull or to tightening caused by drying out. | It becomes impossible to undo as soon as it gets wet. | It can only be adjusted by applying strong tension to the standing part.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Intérêts, when not under tension it is easy to undo even when wet and after a strong pull.

stem: According to the text, how does the bowline react when tension increases?
choices: The stronger the tension, the more it locks, while still untying just as easily. | The stronger the tension, the more it tends to slip and come undone. | Tension has no effect on its locking, which depends on an intermediate mechanical system.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Intérêts, the stronger the tension, the more the knot locks and the more easily it unties.

stem: According to the text, the bowline is also called:
choices: Bowline knot (nœud de bouline). | Clove hitch. | Hangman's knot.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Présentation, the bowline is also called the nœud de bouline.

stem: Why is the bowline described as the king of knots, according to the text?
choices: It is a reliable loop knot that does not slip under tension and unties easily even after strong tension. | It is the only knot that is impossible to untie once tightened. | It can only be made with a specialized tool and with two hands.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Roi des nœuds, its quality as a reliable loop knot that does not slip and unties easily justifies it.
source_ref: Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Roi des nœuds

stem: Why is the bowline described as the “king of knots”?
choices: It is a reliable loop knot that does not slip under tension and unties easily | It tightens more and more under tension until it becomes impossible to untie | It is used only to join two ropes of the same diameter
explanation: The bowline is a reliable loop that does not slip and unties easily.
source_ref: Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Roi des nœuds

stem: Regarding the methods for making the bowline, which statement(s) does the text confirm?
choices: There are several methods for making it, up to 18 including the variants. | Only two classic methods are recognized for teaching, knitted or prepared. | A single unique method of making it is accepted throughout the world.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Roi des nœuds, there are up to 18 methods including variants but two classic teaching methods.
source_ref: Matelotage — Nœud de chaise : Roi des nœuds

stem: According to the text, what does the hangman's knot illustrate regarding bight knots?
choices: It can be easily undone if the loop is free, but tightens around the restraint under the weight of the hanged person. | It can never be undone, whether the loop is free or restrained. | It loosens automatically as soon as weight is applied to the loop.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud (lien) : Ganses, the hangman's knot comes undone if the loop is free but tightens under the weight of the hanged person.

stem: According to the text, what is a bight (ganse)?
choices: A curved portion of rope, with no crossing or overlapping. | A tight crossing of two rope ends. | A rope braided from three metal strands.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud (lien) : Ganses, a bight is a curved portion of rope with no crossing or overlapping.

stem: According to the text, what is the strength of a rope fitted with a bowline compared to a clove hitch?
choices: The bowline retains an exceptional strength of 89%, whereas the clove hitch halves it. | The clove hitch retains 89% of the strength, against half for the bowline. | Both knots reduce the rope's strength by exactly half.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud (lien) : Propriétés, the clove hitch reduces strength by half whereas the bowline reaches 89%.

stem: According to the text, which criterion makes it possible to evaluate and choose the most suitable knot?
choices: The strength of the knot, in particular whether or not it weakens the rope in case of violent jerks. | The color of the rope used to make it. | The purchase price of the cord.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud (lien) : Propriétés, the strength of the knot is paramount, especially in case of violent jerks.

stem: According to the text, which statement about the use of knots is correct?
choices: The art of knots mainly developed in sailing vessels and then in sport or leisure boating. | Knots have never been used outside the maritime domain. | Knowledge of knots has become completely useless since the appearance of specialized equipment.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud (lien) : Présentation, knots developed in sailing vessels and then in boating.

stem: According to the text, what is a knot?
choices: The intertwining or interlacing of one or more ropes or other flexible, thread-like objects. | Exclusively the interlacing of two rigid metal cables. | An assembly of planks intended to reinforce a hull.
explanation: According to Matelotage — Nœud (lien) : Présentation, a knot is the intertwining or interlacing of flexible, thread-like objects.

stem: Among these statements about la bise, which one is correct?
choices: Its speed is between 3 and 9 Bf, and both its onset and its end are gradual. | It arises abruptly, like the föhn, and always precedes rain. | Summer bises last longer than winter ones.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: La bise, its speed ranges from 3 to 9 Bf, and its onset and end are gradual (unlike the föhn).

stem: Which pressure situation causes la bise to set in?
choices: High pressure over the British Isles and low pressure over Italy. | Low pressure over the British Isles and high pressure over Italy. | High pressure over Italy and low pressure over Savoy.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: La bise, it sets in when high pressure covers the British Isles and low pressure covers Italy.

stem: During which period is the Bise clearly dominant on Lake Geneva?
choices: In the first half of the year, particularly in spring | In high summer, from July to August | Only during autumn nights
explanation: MeteoSwiss: the Bise is dominant in the first half of the year, especially in spring.

stem: Which statement is correct about the storm vaudaire?
choices: It is cold: as soon as it appears, the temperature drops and the humidity rises. | It is warm and dry, raising the temperature as soon as it appears. | It occurs exclusively from December to March.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: La vaudaire, the storm vaudaire is cold, with a drop in temperature and a rise in humidity as soon as it appears.

stem: Concerning the two types of vaudaire, which of these statements is FALSE?
choices: The föhn vaudaire appears mainly in March-April as well as in October-November. | The storm vaudaire appears from April to September. | The föhn vaudaire is a cold, humid wind laden with precipitation.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: La vaudaire, the föhn vaudaire comes back down dried out and warmed after having shed its humidity on the southern side of the Alps.

stem: What are the warning signs that announce le Bornan?
choices: The build-up of large cumulus and cumulonimbus clouds over the high ground, particularly the Savoy Prealps. | A perfectly clear and stable sky above the Dent d'Oche. | The appearance of a roll cloud bordering the Jura.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: Le Bornan, the build-up of cumulus and cumulonimbus clouds over the high ground, especially the Savoy Prealps, is its warning sign.

stem: Which statements are correct about le Bornan?
choices: It can reach 30 to 40 kt and its effects are felt mainly between Rolle and Lutry. | It is one of the most violent storm winds of Lake Geneva, occurring in summer. | It is a cold winter wind channelled by the Rhône valley.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: Le Bornan, it reaches 30 to 40 kt between Rolle and Lutry and is one of the most violent summer storm winds of Lake Geneva.

stem: From which direction and origin does le joran come?
choices: It is a NW wind, relatively cold, that comes down from the Jura towards Lake Geneva. | It is a SE wind, warm, that comes up from the Rhône valley. | It is a NE wind that blows from the Vaud Prealps.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: Le joran, the joran is a relatively cold NW wind that comes down from the Jura towards Lake Geneva.

stem: How do the storm joran and the joran that rises after a cold front compare?
choices: The storm joran is often dangerous because it is sudden and sometimes violent. | The gusts of the cold-front joran are clearly stronger than those of the storm joran. | The storm joran blows steadily and is appreciated by sailors.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: Le joran, the storm joran is sudden and sometimes violent, hence often dangerous, unlike the steady cold-front joran.

stem: What is the origin of the alternating breezes observed near lakes?
choices: The difference in temperature between the water, warmer at night, and the land, warmer during the day. | The arrival of an Atlantic disturbance over Lake Geneva. | The channelling of cold air coming down from the Jura.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: Les brises, they originate from the difference in temperature between the water (warmer at night) and the land (warmer during the day).

stem: Which statements are correct about daytime and nighttime breezes?
choices: Daytime breezes blow from the lake towards the shore. | Nighttime breezes generally rise between 5 and 7 p.m. | Nighttime breezes blow from the shore towards the lake and are very violent winds.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: Les brises, daytime breezes blow from the lake towards the shore and nighttime ones (weak winds) rise between 5 and 7 p.m.

stem: Which weather situation does Le Vent (sudois) correspond to?
choices: Low pressure over the British Isles and high pressure over southern Europe. | High pressure over the British Isles and low pressure over southern Europe. | A NE bise channelled between the Jura and the Prealps.
explanation: According to MétéoSuisse: Le Vent, it corresponds to low pressure over the British Isles and high pressure over southern Europe.

stem: What does this signal mean?
choices: Boat landing area | The width of the passage is limited | Strong wind warning
explanation: ONI Annexe 4 – fig. 61 — « Strong wind warning ».

stem: What does this signal mean?
choices: Storm warning | Dam | Strong wind warning
explanation: ONI Annexe 4 – fig. 62 — « Storm warning ».

stem: What does this light signal mean?
choices: Steady light visible from all sides | Quick-flashing light | Blue quick-flashing light
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 1 — "Steady light visible from all sides".

stem: At night, which lights must a sailing vessel under motor (sails hoisted or not) carry?
choices: A white masthead light, green and red side lights, and a white stern light | Only an ordinary white light visible from all sides | A white masthead light and a tricolour light combining the side lights and the stern light at the top of the mast
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 10 — white masthead light, green/red side lights, white stern light.

stem: A sailing vessel under motor may combine its side lights and its stern light into a single tricolour light. Where is this tricolour light placed, and which other light is still required?
choices: A white masthead light, with the tricolour light placed at the top of the mast or near it | A white masthead light, with the tricolour light placed on the stern at water level | No masthead light, and a white stern light separate from the tricolour light
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 11 — white masthead light; side lights and stern light combined into a tricolour light at the top of the mast or near it.

stem: At night, which light must a non-motorised vessel sailing alone or in a towed convoy carry?
choices: An ordinary white light visible from all sides | A white masthead light with green and red side lights | An ordinary yellow light visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 14 — non-motorised vessels, alone or towed: ordinary white light visible from all sides.

stem: At night, which light must a vessel under sail (without motor) carry as a minimum?
choices: An ordinary white light visible from all sides | Green and red side lights and a white stern light | A white masthead light and a tricolour light at the top of the mast
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 15 — vessels under sail: ordinary white light visible from all sides.

stem: What does this light signal mean?
choices: Flashing light | Fixed light visible from all sides | Tricolour light placed at the top of the mast or near it
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 17 — « Tricolour light placed at the top of the mast or near it ».

stem: At night, a vessel carries two superimposed lights visible from all sides, a stern light and a bicolour light (or separate side lights). What are the colours of the two superimposed lights?
choices: The upper light red, the lower light green | The upper light green, the lower light red | Both lights white
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 18 — two superimposed lights visible from all sides: the upper red, the lower green.

stem: What does this light signal mean?
choices: Blue quick-flashing light | Steady light visible from all sides | Quick-flashing light
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 2 — "Quick-flashing light".

stem: At night, when the safety of navigation requires it, how must floating equipment be marked?
choices: By lighting that allows its outline to be made out | By an ordinary yellow light visible from all sides | By a white masthead light and green and red side lights
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 20 — floating equipment: when safety requires it, lighting that allows the outline to be made out.

stem: In addition to the usual navigation lights, which distinctive light must a priority vessel carry at night?
choices: A bright green light visible from all sides, placed at least 1 m higher than the masthead light | A bright red light visible from all sides, placed at least 1 m higher than the masthead light | An ordinary yellow light visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 21 — priority vessels: a bright green light visible from all sides, at least 1 m above the masthead light.

stem: What does this sign mean?
choices: No mooring | Green ball | The clearance height of the passage is limited
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 22 — "Green ball".

stem: To indicate that it must be protected against wash, which additional lights does a vessel carry at night, in addition to the prescribed lights?
choices: An ordinary red light visible from all sides above an ordinary white light visible from all sides | An ordinary white light above an ordinary red light, both visible from all sides | Two superimposed ordinary red lights visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 23 — protection against wash: red light visible from all sides above a white light visible from all sides.

stem: What does this signal mean?
choices: A flag whose upper half is red and lower half white | Swinging a red flag | Two superimposed white flags
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 24 — « A flag whose upper half is red and lower half white ».

stem: What does this signal mean?
choices: Two flags, the upper red and the lower white | Two superimposed white flags | Swinging a red flag
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 25 — « Two flags, the upper red and the lower white ».

stem: What does this sign mean?
choices: Mandatory stop under certain conditions | Two white flags one above the other | Anchoring permitted
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 27 — "Two white flags one above the other".

stem: What does this sign mean?
choices: Yellow ball | Police boats and boats of other services | Yellow buoys marking each anchor

explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 31 — "Blue quick-flashing light".

stem: Which signal does a vessel of the police, the border guard or the fisheries inspectorate hoist when it wants to make contact with another vessel?

explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 32 — letter «K» flag, half on the staff side yellow, the other half blue.

stem: At night, which light must a professional fishing vessel carry?
choices: An ordinary yellow light visible from all sides | An ordinary white light visible from all sides | An ordinary red light visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 33 — professional fishing vessels: ordinary yellow light visible from all sides.

stem: What does this sign mean?
choices: Pass only in the direction indicated | Yellow ball | Mandatory stop under certain conditions
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 34 — "Yellow ball".

stem: Which daytime signal must a vessel trolling for fish display?
choices: A white ball | A green ball visible from all sides | An ordinary yellow light visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 35 — vessels trolling for fish: white ball.

stem: In the case of underwater diving carried out from the shore, which signal must be displayed?
choices: The letter «A» board (a swallow-tailed pennant, half on the staff side white, the other half blue) | The letter «K» flag (half on the staff side yellow, the other half blue) | A white ball visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 36 — diving from the shore: letter «A» board, white/blue swallow-tailed pennant.

stem: In the case of underwater diving carried out from a body of water, how must the letter «A» board be displayed?
choices: The letter «A» board (white/blue pennant) visible from all sides | The letter «A» board facing only towards the shore | A red flag visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 37 — diving from a body of water: letter «A» board (white/blue pennant) visible from all sides.

stem: What does this sign mean?
choices: Waving a red flag | The width of the passage is limited | Pass only in the direction indicated
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 39 — "Waving a red flag".

stem: At night, which lights must a motorised vessel sailing alone (or a tug) carry?
choices: A bright white masthead (or bow) light, bright green and bright red side lights, and an ordinary white stern light | Only an ordinary white light visible from all sides | A bright white light and a bright green light visible from all sides placed higher than the masthead light
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 4 — motorised vessels alone/tugs: bright white masthead light, bright green/red side lights, ordinary white stern light.

stem: At night, how does a vessel in distress signal its situation?
choices: By swinging a light in a circular motion | By swinging a light (without a circular motion) | By showing two fixed superimposed red lights
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 40 — vessels in distress: swing a light in a circular motion.

stem: What does this signal mean?
choices: Swinging a red flag | Swinging a red flag, or any other suitable object, in a circular motion | Two superimposed white flags
explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 41 — « Swinging a red flag, or any other suitable object, in a circular motion ».

stem: What does this signal mean?

explanation: ONI Annexe 2 – fig. 42 — « A slow, repeated up-and-down movement of the arms extended to each side ».

stem: At night, how does a piece of floating equipment (or a vessel at work, aground or sunk) indicate the sides on which passage is clear?
choices: By an ordinary red light and an ordinary white light | By an ordinary red light alone | By an ordinary green light and an ordinary white light
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 43 — side where passage is clear: ordinary red light + ordinary white light; side not clear: ordinary red light alone.

stem: By day, which flag indicates the side on which passage is NOT clear near a piece of floating equipment or a vessel at work?
choices: A red flag | A flag whose upper half is red and lower half white | An entirely white flag
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 44 — side where passage is not clear: red flag; side clear: red/white flag.

stem: By day, which signal indicates the side on which passage is clear near a piece of floating equipment or a vessel at work (two-flag variant)?
choices: Two superimposed flags, the upper red and the lower white | Two superimposed flags, the upper white and the lower red | A single red flag
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 45 — side clear: two superimposed flags, upper red, lower white; side not clear: red flag.

stem: At night, where is the masthead light (bright white light) of a pushed convoy placed?
choices: On the leading vessel | On the pushing vessel, at the rear of the convoy | At the centre of the convoy, on the most visible vessel
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 5 — pushed convoys: bright white masthead light placed on the leading vessel.

stem: At night, which lights may a professional fishing, sport or motorised pleasure vessel (or a sailing vessel under motor) carry under this simplified variant?
choices: A white light visible from all sides and green and red side lights | A white masthead light, green/red side lights and a separate white stern light | Only an ordinary white light visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 8 — white light visible from all sides + green and red side lights.

stem: Under this variant, how may the side lights of a motorised sport/pleasure vessel (or sailing vessel under motor) carrying a white light visible from all sides be arranged in the forward area?
choices: Side by side in the forward area, or as a bicolour light | Combined with the stern light into a tricolour light at the top of the mast | Replaced by a single ordinary yellow light visible from all sides
explanation: ONI Annexe 2, fig. 9 — white light visible from all sides + green/red side lights, placed side by side in the forward area or as a bicolour light.

explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 46 — example: obligation not to exceed 12 km/h at 1000 m.

stem: How is an isolated obstacle marked?

explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 53 — channel in a shoal area: right side (seen from open water) green cones, left side red cylinders.

explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 54 — North quadrant: two cones one above the other, both points up.

stem: Cardinal marks indicate an extended shoal by indicating the North and West quadrants. What do they indicate?

explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 55 — the marks indicate that the deep water lies in the North and West quadrants.

stem: At night, which lights must a sailing vessel under motor (sails hoisted or not) carry?
choices: A white masthead light, green and red side lights, and a white stern light | A white light visible from all sides and green and red side lights, without a masthead light | Only an ordinary white light visible from all sides
explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 10 — white masthead light, green and red side lights, white stern light.

stem: A sailing vessel under motor may, instead of separate side lights and a stern light, combine these lights. How and where is this combined light arranged, in addition to the white masthead light?
choices: A tricolour light combining the side lights and the stern light, placed at the top of the mast or near it | A bicolour light placed in the forward area, with the stern light remaining separate | A white light visible from all sides placed at the stern
explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 11 — white masthead light and tricolour light (sides + stern) at the top of the mast or near it.

stem: At night, which lights must a vessel or floating equipment being towed carry?

explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 14 — ordinary white masthead light and ordinary white stern light.

explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 17 — ordinary white light visible from all sides.

explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 18 — green and red side lights, white stern light (no masthead light).

stem: What does this light signal mean?

explanation: RNL Annexe I – fig. 19 — "Tricolour light placed at the top of the mast or near it".

stem: What does this signal mean?

stem: At night, how does a vessel that has become unable to manoeuvre signal its situation, in addition to its ordinary lights?

explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 22 — two red lights one above the other about 1 m apart, visible from all sides.

stem: At night, a vessel carrying out works or sounding operations: what does it show on the side where passage is free, and on the side where it is not free?
